skill.name: relight
skill.description: Relight still images through RunComfy by changing lighting direction, color temperature, intensity, and mood while preserving the subject.
skill.summary: Use RunComfy image-editing models to relight still images with controlled lighting prompts.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer overcounted Markdown backticks and ordinary links as shell execution and malware indicators. Human review confirms legitimate but elevated behavior: the skill runs the RunComfy CLI, sends user images and prompts to RunComfy network endpoints, writes output files, and stores an API token in a local config path. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: RunComfy CLI Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill authorizes Bash(runcomfy *) and provides runcomfy login and run commands. This is expected for the skill, but it means an agent can execute a third-party CLI that performs network work and writes files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command surface is explicit in allowed-tools and examples. The risk is legitimate and bounded to runcomfy commands, with no evidence of arbitrary shell expansion.

medium_findings.title: User Images and Prompts Sent to External Service
medium_findings.description: The skill states that the CLI posts to the RunComfy Model API and uses remote image URLs. This can expose user-provided images, prompts, and generated outputs to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is directly documented and central to the skill. It is not malicious by itself, but users need a clear privacy warning.

medium_findings.title: Local API Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token to a hidden user config path. The file mode is stated as 0600, but token storage remains sensitive and should be handled carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact token path and permission claim are documented in the skill. The finding is sensitive but expected for authenticated CLI use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static external_commands hits are Markdown inline code, model identifiers, example prompts, or fenced command examples. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or hidden dynamic command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose, Markdown formatting, or user-facing examples. They are not executable Ruby code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Mostly Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Static network hits include homepage, documentation, catalog, and related skill links. These links support user documentation, while the real outbound processing risk is separately captured.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and catalog references. They do not show credential exfiltration or covert telemetry.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Hits Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The high blocker and reconnaissance patterns appear to be scanner misclassification of prose, model names, links, and exit-code documentation. No cryptographic implementation or system scanning behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe model selection and CLI troubleshooting. There is no code that performs cryptography or reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permitted External CLI Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows runcomfy commands through Bash(runcomfy *). This is a required capability, but it should remain limited to the documented RunComfy CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission is explicit in the skill front matter. The scope appears restricted to runcomfy commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Processing of User Media
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill routes relighting jobs to RunComfy endpoints and remote model APIs. Users should avoid sending confidential images unless they trust the service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound API behavior is explicitly documented. The endpoints are declared, not hidden.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Token File
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents API token storage under ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json. Users should protect this file and prefer scoped tokens when available.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden config file path is named directly. The risk is standard credential handling rather than evidence of abuse.

content.value_statement: Changing lighting after a photo shoot is slow and inconsistent with manual editing. This skill routes still images to RunComfy relighting models with prompts for direction, color, intensity, and mood.
actual_capabilities: Relights still images with the Qwen Edit 2509 Relight LoRA endpoint.
Chooses fallback image-edit models when relighting is part of a broader edit.
Provides RunComfy CLI commands for single-image relighting jobs.
Supports prompts for light direction, color temperature, intensity, and mood.
Explains preservation prompts for subject pose, framing, and color identity.
Documents common patterns for product, portrait, landscape, and reference-based relighting.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy login or token.
Sends images and prompts to RunComfy network services for processing.
Targets still images only, not video relighting.
Results can drift if the prompt does not state what must be preserved.

use_cases.title: Relight product catalog images
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent studio or window-light look across product photos while preserving orientation and color.

use_cases.title: Shift portrait mood
use_cases.description: Change overcast portraits to golden hour, blue hour, or studio lighting while keeping identity and pose stable.

use_cases.title: Test lighting directions
use_cases.description: Compare left key light, rim light, top-down light, and soft fill before committing to final edits.

prompt_templates.title: Basic relight
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight this image with warm golden hour light from camera-left. Preserve the subject, pose, framing, and main colors.

prompt_templates.title: Product lighting pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight this product as soft studio light with a large key light from the left and gentle shadow on the right. Preserve packaging color and orientation.

prompt_templates.title: Portrait mood control
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the face, hair, clothing, pose, and background unchanged. Relight with warm front-left key light, subtle fill, and a soft rim light from behind.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced reference match
prompt_templates.scenario: Match lighting from a reference image
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the lighting direction, color temperature, contrast, and shadow softness from the reference image to the source image. Preserve source identity and composition.

output_examples.input: A product photo on a white background needs warmer lifestyle lighting.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects the relight endpoint and prepares a prompt for warm window light, soft side shadow, and preserved product colors.

output_examples.input: A portrait should look like it was shot at golden hour.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a relighting prompt with warm key light, controlled rim light, and explicit identity preservation.

output_examples.input: A scene needs a broader edit plus a lighting change.
output_examples.output: The assistant routes to a general image-edit model and includes lighting instructions with the other edit requirements.

best_practices: State the light source, color temperature, direction, intensity, and preservation requirements in one prompt.
Use the dedicated relight endpoint when lighting is the main change.
Use only image URLs that the user explicitly provided for the current task.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential images to external services without user approval.
Do not use vague prompts such as make it better when precise lighting is needed.
Do not mix relighting with unrelated object edits unless a broader edit model is selected.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It relights existing still images and points users to separate generation workflows for new images.

faq.question: Which model does it prefer?
faq.answer: It prefers Qwen Edit 2509 Relight LoRA for dedicated relighting tasks.

faq.question: Can it relight video?
faq.answer: No. The documented CLI flow is for still images only.

faq.question: Does it preserve the subject?
faq.answer: It prompts for subject, pose, framing, and color preservation, but results can still vary.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. The RunComfy CLI sends jobs to RunComfy model endpoints for processing.

faq.question: Is an API token required?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need runcomfy login or a RUNCOMFY_TOKEN value for authenticated CLI use.
